verses.text: The history of the synods held by Damasus seems hopelessly obscure, and the date of our encyclical is correspondingly doubtful.

verses.text: Accordingly our encyclical falls between 368 and 372, probably as soon as Damasus had been able to assemble so large a synod, and Athanasius to write in reply (§10).

verses.book: Synodal Letter to the Bishops of Africa. (Ad Afros Epistola Synodica.)
verses.text: It may be added that the letter of the Damasine synod of 372 refers in ambiguous terms to the condemnation of Auxentius as having already taken place, (‘damnatum esse liquet:’ was this because they felt unable to dislodge him? see Tillem. viii.

verses.book: Synodal Letter to the Bishops of Africa. (Ad Afros Epistola Synodica.)

verses.book: Synodal Letter to the Bishops of Africa. (Ad Afros Epistola Synodica.)
verses.text: The head and centre of the dying efforts of Arianism in the Roman West was apparently Auxentius, ‘one of the last survivors of the victory of Ariminum.’

verses.book: Synodal Letter to the Bishops of Africa. (Ad Afros Epistola Synodica.)
verses.text: That he should be still undisturbed in his see, while working far and wide to the damage of the Catholic cause, was to Athanasius a distressing surprise, and he was urging the Western bishops to put an end to such an anomaly.

verses.book: Synodal Letter to the Bishops of Africa. (Ad Afros Epistola Synodica.)
verses.text: In the encyclical before us he begins (1–3) by contrasting the synod of Nicæa with that of Ariminum, and pointing out the real history of the latter, going over again to some extent the ground of the earlier sections of the de Synodis.

verses.book: Synodal Letter to the Bishops of Africa. (Ad Afros Epistola Synodica.)
verses.text: He touches (3. end) on the disastrous termination of the Council.

verses.book: Synodal Letter to the Bishops of Africa. (Ad Afros Epistola Synodica.)
verses.text: He then proceeds to vindicate the Nicene creed (4–8) as essentially Scriptural, i.e. as the only possible bar to the unscriptural formulæ of the Arians.

verses.book: Synodal Letter to the Bishops of Africa. (Ad Afros Epistola Synodica.)
verses.text: This he illustrates (5, 6) by an account, substantially identical with that in the de Decretis, of the evasions of every other test by the Asian bishops at Nicæa.

verses.book: Synodal Letter to the Bishops of Africa. (Ad Afros Epistola Synodica.)
verses.text: He repeatedly urges that the formula was no invention of the Nicene Fathers (6, 9), appealing to the admission of Eusebius to this effect.

verses.book: Synodal Letter to the Bishops of Africa. (Ad Afros Epistola Synodica.)
verses.text: He attacks the Homœan position, shewing that its characteristic watchword merely dissembles the alternative between Anomœanism and the true co-essentiality of the Son (7).

verses.book: Synodal Letter to the Bishops of Africa. (Ad Afros Epistola Synodica.)
verses.text: The most novel argument in the Letter is that of §4, where he refutes the repudiation of οὐσία and ὑπόστασις in the creed of Niké by an argument from Scripture, starting from Ex. iii.

verses.book: Synodal Letter to the Bishops of Africa. (Ad Afros Epistola Synodica.)
verses.text: The completeness of the work of Athanasius, now very near his end, in winning over all Egypt to unanimity in faith and in personal attachment to himself, is quaintly reflected in the naive assurance (§10) that the bishops of Egypt and the Libyas ‘are all of one mind, and we always sign for one another if any chance not to be present.’
